summary: Gruber dismantles a Wall Street Journal report claiming Apple is 'breaking its own rules' with its upcoming headset, arguing the reporters ignore Apple's well-established pattern for new product launches. He points out that neither iPhone nor Apple Watch were instant mass-market hits, and that the WSJ's own past reporting documented Apple Watch skepticism a year after launch. He also highlights that Apple executives explicitly distanced themselves from the 'metaverse' framing at a WSJ conference, making it ironic that WSJ reporters still use that framing. Gruber argues the lack of success from existing XR headsets represents an opportunity for Apple, not a warning sign.
key_points: Apple has never launched two new products the same way, and the WSJ's claim that Apple is 'breaking its rules' misunderstands a pattern that doesn't exist
Apple's actual new-product pattern is consistent: expensive first version, early adopter phase, media declares it a dud, then iterative improvement leads to massive success
The WSJ frames Apple's headset as a metaverse play despite Apple's own executive explicitly rejecting that term at a WSJ conference
AirPods are arguably a major new product category and the first true mass-market AR device, contradicting the claim that the headset is Apple's first major product in a decade
The failure of existing XR headsets signals opportunity for Apple, not doom
money_quotes: Neither Apple Watch nor iPhone 'quickly became consumer must-haves.'

Years later, when Apple is poised to release another altogether new product, the last one — the one written off as a dud after its first year — is held up as a product that was universally hailed as a sensation and smash hit from day one.
The fact that people don't use or even know what the hell the metaverse means is irrelevant to Apple's headset.
It's enough to make you think Wall Street Journal reporters don't read The Wall Street Journal.
That no XR headset introduced so far has made much of a dent in the universe isn't a sign that Apple's effort is ill-fated. It's a sign that Apple has an opportunity.
Apple does have one tradition when entering a new product category: they endeavor to make the first one good enough to be criticized.
I'd argue that AirPods Pro are the first true mass-market augmented reality devices. The fact that they're audio-only, and not visual, blinds people to the fact that they're remarkable AR products.
key_insight: The business press repeatedly fails to recognize Apple's consistent new-product playbook — expensive and limited at launch, then iteratively improved into a hit — and instead rewrites history to claim each prior product was an instant success while declaring the current one a failure.
